title: React Mastery Encyclopedia
subtitle: React 19.2 from components and state to async UI, server boundaries, performance, and interviews

sections.intro: React is a UI runtime built around pure rendering, explicit state, and composition.

blocks.title: What React Actually Is
blocks.html:
React is a library for building user interfaces by describing what the screen should look like for the current data. Components are pure render functions, state changes schedule new renders, and React coordinates host updates during commit.

In 2026, React core is still not a full framework. Routing, server execution, cache invalidation, and project conventions usually come from frameworks or other libraries built around React.

blocks.title: React core vs framework vs ecosystem

items.label: React core
items.html: Components, JSX, state, context, effects, refs, transitions, Suspense, and the rendering mental model.

items.label: Framework layer
items.html: Routing, bundler integration, server execution, code splitting defaults, deployment conventions, and server-side cache tools.

items.label: Ecosystem layer
items.html: Routers, form libraries, server-state tools, design systems, and testing utilities.

blocks.title: The smallest modern React entry point
blocks.caption: React attaches a root, builds a render tree from components, and commits updates to the host environment.

blocks.title: How a React screen starts existing
steps: Your app creates a root and asks React to render a top-level component tree.
React calls component functions to build a tree of React elements.
React compares the new tree to the previous tree when one exists.
React commits only the host changes that are needed.
Events and state updates trigger the next render cycle.

blocks.title: Trap: treating React as the framework instead of the UI layer
blocks.html: Many interview answers blur React, Next.js, and router or cache libraries together. A mastery-level explanation names the layer clearly before describing the feature.

blocks.title: Drill: identify the layer
blocks.prompt: A teammate says, 'React gives us file-based routing, server-side cache invalidation, and layouts.' What is the precise correction?
blocks.answerHtml: React gives the rendering model and UI primitives. File-based routing, server-side cache invalidation, and layout conventions usually come from a framework such as Next.js, not from React core itself.

blocks.title: Mastery recap
items: React describes UI as a function of state.
React core is not the same thing as a full framework.
The main loop is render tree creation, comparison, and commit.
Strong React explanations separate core, framework, and ecosystem layers.

qa.question: What is declarative UI in React?
qa.answerHtml: Declarative UI means you describe what the screen should look like for the current data, and React figures out how to update the interface to match that description.

qa.question: Is React a library or a framework?
qa.answerHtml: React itself is a UI library. Frameworks such as Next.js build routing, server execution, and deployment conventions on top of React.

qa.question: What does StrictMode do in React?
qa.answerHtml: StrictMode enables extra development-time checks and intentionally stresses some lifecycle behavior so unsafe patterns and side effects become easier to spot before production.

qa.question: Why do React components need to stay pure during render?
qa.answerHtml: Render must stay pure so React can call components predictably, compare trees correctly, and safely re-run work when concurrent rendering or development checks require it.

sections.intro: JSX, React elements, and tree identity explain how React knows what should stay and what should reset.

blocks.title: JSX creates React elements, not DOM nodes
blocks.html:
JSX is syntax for describing React elements. Elements are lightweight objects that tell React what you want to render. Components are functions that return elements. DOM nodes only exist after React commits the result into the host environment.

This distinction matters because React reasons about identity in the render tree before any real DOM mutation happens.

blocks.title: Component vs element vs DOM node

items.label: Component
items.html: A function or class that React can call to produce UI.

items.label: Element
items.html: An object description of what should appear in the tree, such as a host tag or another component with props.

items.label: DOM node
items.html: A real browser node created or updated during commit.

blocks.title: JSX, keys, and conditional structure
blocks.caption: Keys identify sibling positions in the render tree. They are not just there to silence warnings.

blocks.title: How position and keys shape identity
steps: React walks siblings in order when reconciling a list or branch.
Without keys, React falls back to position-based matching.
With stable keys, React can preserve or move the right subtree when order changes.
Changing the key tells React to treat that subtree as a different identity.

blocks.title: Index key bugs are state bugs, not just performance bugs
blocks.html: If you key reorderable items by array index, React may preserve the wrong component state for the wrong row. Inputs, focus, and in-progress UI can appear to move because React thinks the identity stayed attached to the position instead of the item.

blocks.title: Drill: which state survives?
blocks.prompt: You render two tabs with the same component type. In one version you switch them by conditionally rendering the same subtree in the same position. In another version you give each tab panel a distinct key. Which version preserves state and which resets it?
blocks.answerHtml: The same-position version preserves state because React sees the same tree position and component identity. The keyed version resets state because changing the key tells React this is a different subtree.

blocks.title: Mastery recap
items: JSX creates React elements, not DOM nodes.
Components produce elements; commit produces host nodes.
Tree position and keys control identity.
Wrong keys lead to wrong state preservation.

qa.question: What does JSX compile to?
qa.answerHtml: JSX compiles to JavaScript that creates React element objects. It is syntax for describing UI, not a template language the browser understands directly.

qa.question: What is the difference between a React element and a DOM node?
qa.answerHtml: A React element is a lightweight object description of UI. A DOM node is the real browser object created or updated later during commit.

qa.question: Why are keys important in React lists?
qa.answerHtml: Keys help React match sibling items across renders so it can preserve the right subtree state and update the right nodes efficiently.

qa.question: What happens when you use an array index as a key?
qa.answerHtml: Index keys can make state stick to the wrong item when a list is reordered, filtered, or inserted into, because React falls back to position instead of true item identity.

sections.intro: React scales when component APIs stay small, predictable, and composable.

blocks.title: Components are UI functions with explicit inputs
blocks.html:
A React component should be readable as a function of props, state, and context. Props are inputs from the parent. State belongs to the component. Composition lets you build larger interfaces by combining small units without inheritance-heavy APIs.

The strongest component APIs are narrow, predictable, and easy to rearrange in different layouts.

blocks.title: Composition beats prop soup
blocks.caption: The parent controls content through composition instead of forcing the child to own every visual detail.

blocks.title: Composition vs inheritance vs prop soup

items.label: Composition
items.html: Flexible, React-native, and easy to extend through children or slots.

items.label: Inheritance
items.html: Rare in React UI design and usually a sign that behavior is too tightly coupled.

items.label: Prop soup
items.html: A component with many booleans and style toggles becomes hard to reason about and easy to break.

blocks.title: How component boundaries grow in a healthy React app
steps: Start with a small component that owns only the UI and state it truly needs.
Lift state up only when multiple children need to coordinate through a common parent.
Extract composition points like children, render areas, or subcomponents when APIs get crowded.
Move side effects and data orchestration to the nearest component that actually owns them.

blocks.title: Trap: mutating props or mirroring them into state
blocks.html: Props are read-only inputs. If you mutate them, React cannot trust your component model. If you copy props into state without a real ownership reason, you usually create stale duplicates that drift out of sync.

blocks.title: Drill: redesign the API
blocks.prompt: A modal component has props like showHeader, showFooter, primaryLabel, secondaryLabel, danger, icon, tone, fullBleed, and customBodyHtml. What React-oriented redesign should you propose first?
blocks.answerHtml: Shift toward composition. Keep a small shell component such as Modal and pass header, body, footer, or child subcomponents through composition instead of adding more boolean or content props.

blocks.title: Mastery recap
items: Components should read like pure UI functions.
Props are inputs, not mutable storage.
Composition is the default React reuse strategy.
Prop soup is often a boundary-design problem.

qa.question: Why does React prefer composition over inheritance?
qa.answerHtml: Composition keeps UI flexible by letting components be combined instead of forcing behavior into rigid class hierarchies. It matches how React trees naturally grow.

qa.question: What is prop drilling?
qa.answerHtml: Prop drilling is passing data through multiple intermediate components that do not actually use it, just so a deeper child can receive it.

qa.question: What is the children prop in React?
qa.answerHtml: children is the prop that represents nested content inside a component's opening and closing tags. It is a core building block for composition.

qa.question: When would you use a compound component pattern?
qa.answerHtml: Use compound components when several related pieces should share state and expose a flexible, declarative API, such as tabs, accordions, or menus.

sections.intro: Most React bugs come from misunderstanding when code runs and what snapshot of state a handler can see.

blocks.title: Render phase, commit phase, and state snapshots
blocks.html:
React rendering is the process of calling components to figure out what the next UI should be. Commit is when React applies the required host updates. State behaves like a snapshot for a specific render. Calling a setter schedules future work; it does not mutate the current render's local variable.

This is why event handlers often appear to read old state after a setter call. They still belong to the render that created them.

blocks.title: Render vs commit vs effect

items.label: Render
items.html: React calls components and computes the next tree. This must stay pure.

items.label: Commit
items.html: React applies host updates, refs, and DOM changes.

items.label: Effect
items.html: Effects run after commit to synchronize with systems outside React.

blocks.title: Why three setter calls do not read three new values
blocks.caption: The first handler reads one stale snapshot three times. The second composes queued updates from the latest pending value.

blocks.title: What happens after setState
steps: The event handler runs inside the current render's closure.
Calling a state setter queues an update instead of mutating the current variable.
React decides when to render again and which updates to batch together.
During the next render, hooks return values for the new snapshot.
After commit, the screen reflects the result.

blocks.title: Trap: logging state right after the setter
blocks.html: setCount(count + 1); console.log(count); logs the old snapshot because the handler is still running inside the current render. The setter requested the next render; it did not rewrite the current closure.

blocks.title: Drill: predict the final count
blocks.prompt: The current count is 2. A click handler runs setCount(count + 1) twice and then setCount((prev) => prev + 1) once. What count should render next, and why?
blocks.answerHtml: The next count is 4. The first two updates both compute from the same snapshot and both request 3, so they collapse to the same result. The functional update then runs against the latest pending value and adds one more.

blocks.title: Mastery recap
items: Render computes the next tree, commit applies host changes, and Effects synchronize after commit.
State is a snapshot for a specific render.
Setters schedule future renders instead of mutating current local variables.
Functional updates are the safe tool when the next state depends on the previous one.

qa.question: What does 'state is a snapshot' mean in React?
qa.answerHtml: It means each render sees its own fixed view of state. Calling a setter schedules the next render rather than mutating the current render's local value.

qa.question: Why do multiple state updates not always change the value immediately?
qa.answerHtml: Because React queues updates and processes them during the next render cycle instead of mutating state synchronously inside the current handler.

qa.question: When should you use functional state updates?
qa.answerHtml: Use functional updates when the next state depends on the previous state, because React may batch or queue multiple updates together.

qa.question: Why does logging state right after a setter often show the old value?
qa.answerHtml: Because the log still runs inside the current render's closure. The setter requested a future render; it did not rewrite the current snapshot.

sections.intro: State belongs at the lowest level that can truly own it, and React preserves or resets it based on identity.

blocks.title: State ownership and identity
blocks.html:
Choose the smallest component that should truly own a piece of state. Lift state up when multiple children must coordinate through a common parent. Reset state intentionally by changing identity with keys or tree position instead of patching around stale local copies.

React associates state with a component's position in the render tree, not with the JSX text on the screen. That is why two identical-looking tags can preserve different state, and why one tag can unexpectedly keep state you meant to reset.

blocks.title: Same position preserves state, new identity resets it

items.label: Same component at the same position
items.html: React preserves the subtree state because it sees the same identity in the same place.

items.label: Different component type at the same position
items.html: React resets state because the subtree type changed.

items.label: Same component with a different key
items.html: React also resets state because the key says this is a different identity.

blocks.title: Keys as a deliberate reset tool
blocks.caption: Changing the key tells React to discard the old draft state and create a fresh subtree.

blocks.title: How React decides whether to preserve state
steps: React compares the previous and next render trees position by position.
If the component type and key match, React preserves the subtree state.
If the type changes or the key changes, React treats the subtree as new.
Nested component definitions create new identities accidentally and can reset state on every render.

blocks.title: Trap: mirroring props into state to force a reset
blocks.html: Copying a prop into state so you can manually keep it in sync usually creates stale duplicates. If the real goal is to reset UI when identity changes, use better ownership or change the subtree identity with keys.

blocks.title: Drill: when should this form reset?
blocks.prompt: A chat composer preserves the previous recipient's draft when you switch from Alice to Bob because the same Composer stays in the same place. What is the clean React fix if you want a fresh draft per recipient switch?
blocks.answerHtml: Give the composer a key tied to the recipient identity, such as <Composer key={recipient.id} recipient={recipient} />. That tells React to reset the subtree when the recipient changes.

blocks.title: Mastery recap
items: Keep state local until sharing forces you to lift it.
State is attached to identity in the render tree.
Same type plus same position usually preserves state.
Keys are a deliberate preservation and reset tool.

qa.question: What is lifting state up?
qa.answerHtml: Lifting state up means moving shared state to the nearest common parent so multiple children can coordinate through one source of truth.

qa.question: When does React preserve state and when does it reset it?
qa.answerHtml: React preserves state when the same component identity stays in the same tree position. It resets state when the type changes, the key changes, or the subtree identity is replaced.

qa.question: How can a key be used to intentionally reset state?
qa.answerHtml: Changing a component's key tells React to treat it as a different identity, so the old subtree is discarded and a fresh one is mounted.

qa.question: How do you decide where state should live?
qa.answerHtml: Keep state in the smallest component that truly owns it, then lift or share it only when multiple parts of the UI need to coordinate around the same value.

sections.intro: Events represent user intent. Effects synchronize React with systems outside React.

blocks.title: Events are not Effects
blocks.html:
Put user intent in event handlers. Use Effects only when synchronizing React with timers, subscriptions, browser APIs, or other external systems. Effects are an escape hatch from the normal React model, not the default place to put business logic.

Reach for useEffectEvent when an Effect needs the latest callback or value without forcing the Effect to tear down and recreate a subscription every time a render happens.

blocks.title: You might not need an Effect

items.label: Transforming data for render
items.html: Usually compute it during render instead of storing a derived copy in state.

items.label: Responding to a user action
items.html: Usually keep it inside the event handler that already knows what happened.

items.label: Synchronizing with an external system
items.html: This is the real home for an Effect.

blocks.title: Derive during render instead of syncing with an Effect
blocks.caption: If no external system is involved, storing a second piece of derived state usually creates unnecessary work and more chances for bugs.

blocks.title: Effect lifecycle and dependency reasoning
steps: Render computes what the UI should look like.
Commit applies host updates.
React runs the Effect after commit to synchronize with the external system.
If a dependency changes, React cleans up the old synchronization and runs the new one.
If the Effect only existed to patch derived state, you often created work React never needed.

blocks.title: Trap: fixing event logic with an Effect
blocks.html: If a click should submit analytics, update state, or call a mutation, do it in the click handler. Moving that logic into an Effect usually disconnects cause from intent and makes the code harder to reason about.

blocks.title: Drill: should this be an Effect or an event?
blocks.prompt: A form has submitted state. An Effect watches submitted and posts analytics when it becomes true. What is the cleaner React design?
blocks.answerHtml: Post analytics in the submit handler where the user action actually happens. The Effect is unnecessary because no external synchronization needs to be derived from future renders.

blocks.title: Mastery recap
items: If no external system is involved, you may not need an Effect.
User intent belongs in event handlers.
Effects synchronize React with systems outside React.
useEffectEvent helps Effects read the latest values without needless resubscription.

qa.question: What is the difference between an event handler and an Effect in React?
qa.answerHtml: An event handler responds to a specific user action. An Effect synchronizes React with something outside React after rendering has committed.

qa.question: When should you avoid useEffect?
qa.answerHtml: Avoid it for derived data, ordinary event logic, or UI resets that better ownership or keys can model directly. Effects are for external synchronization.

qa.question: What is useEffectEvent for?
qa.answerHtml: It lets an Effect call logic that always sees the latest props and state without forcing the whole Effect to resubscribe whenever those values change.

qa.question: What is the difference between useEffect and useLayoutEffect?
qa.answerHtml: Both synchronize with external systems, but useLayoutEffect runs earlier around layout and paint timing. Use it only when visual measurement or paint-sensitive work truly requires it.

sections.intro: Refs hold mutable values across renders without causing re-renders and act as the main bridge to imperative browser work.

blocks.title: Refs are mutable cells, not reactive state
blocks.html:
Use refs for DOM access, timers, external instance handles, and values that should survive renders without painting the screen again. If changing the value should update the UI, it belongs in state instead.

Refs are an escape hatch. They are useful because React keeps them across renders, but changing ref.current does not trigger a re-render.

blocks.title: useRef vs state

items.label: Ref
items.html: Mutable, survives renders, and does not trigger a re-render when changed.

items.label: State
items.html: Participates in React rendering and schedules a re-render when updated.

items.label: Rule of thumb
items.html: If the value affects what the user sees, prefer state. If it only supports handlers or imperative coordination, a ref may be right.

blocks.title: Using refs for focus and imperative DOM work
blocks.caption: Refs are the normal bridge for focus, selection, measurement, timers, and external instance handles.

blocks.title: How refs behave across renders
steps: React creates the ref object once and keeps the same object between renders.
Updating ref.current mutates that object immediately.
Because React does not track ref.current as render state, no new render is scheduled.
When attached to a DOM node, React writes the node into ref.current during commit and resets it to null on removal.

blocks.title: Trap: hiding visible UI state inside a ref
blocks.html: If the user should see the updated value, do not hide it in a ref just to avoid rendering. That creates UI that can drift from the real data because React has no idea the visible value changed.

blocks.title: Drill: should this value live in a ref or state?
blocks.prompt: A stopwatch needs to display elapsed seconds and also keep track of an interval ID so it can stop the timer later. Which value belongs in state, and which belongs in a ref?
blocks.answerHtml: The displayed elapsed time belongs in state because the UI must re-render when it changes. The interval ID belongs in a ref because handlers need it, but changing it should not paint the screen.

blocks.title: Mastery recap
items: Refs persist across renders.
Refs do not trigger re-renders.
Use refs for imperative coordination, not visible state.
DOM access through refs happens during commit, not during ordinary pure rendering.

qa.question: What is useRef used for?
qa.answerHtml: useRef stores a mutable value that persists across renders without causing a re-render when it changes. It is commonly used for DOM nodes, timers, and external instances.

qa.question: What is the difference between refs and state?
qa.answerHtml: Refs store mutable values outside the render cycle, while state participates in rendering and triggers a re-render when updated.

qa.question: When do you use refs for DOM work?
qa.answerHtml: Use refs when you need imperative DOM tasks like focus, selection, measurement, scrolling, or integrating with third-party widgets.

qa.question: What is useImperativeHandle used for?
qa.answerHtml: It lets a component customize the imperative handle exposed through a ref, so a parent can call a small safe API instead of reaching into internal DOM details.

sections.intro: Context solves value distribution, reducers model transitions, and external stores need a React-safe subscription boundary.

blocks.title: Context, reducers, and external-store boundaries
blocks.html:
Use Context for low-frequency shared values, reducers for explicit state transitions, and useSyncExternalStore when React must subscribe to data that lives outside React. These tools solve different problems and get confused when one is stretched to do all of them.

Context is about distribution through the tree. Reducers are about making state transitions explicit. External stores are about subscribing safely to state that React does not own.

blocks.title: Context vs reducer vs external store

items.label: Context
items.html: Good for values many descendants need, especially when updates are not extremely frequent.

items.label: Reducer
items.html: Good when one domain has multiple state transitions and you want explicit action-based updates.

items.label: External store
items.html: Good when state already lives outside React or when cross-tree subscriptions and selective updates need a dedicated store model.

blocks.title: useSyncExternalStore
blocks.caption: useSyncExternalStore gives React a stable subscription contract for data it does not own.

blocks.title: How to choose the boundary
steps: Start with local state in the component that truly owns the concern.
Lift or distribute values with Context when multiple descendants need the same low-frequency data.
Use a reducer when updates become action-driven and complex enough to benefit from explicit transitions.
Use an external store when the data already lives outside React or when many subscribers need selective updates.

blocks.title: Trap: using one giant Context for everything
blocks.html: A large Context value that changes often can force wide re-renders and blur domain boundaries. Split Context by responsibility or move to a dedicated external store when subscription granularity matters.

blocks.title: Drill: which boundary is correct?
blocks.prompt: An app has theme, auth session, a live websocket feed, and a complex checkout state machine. Which concerns fit Context, reducers, or an external store best?
blocks.answerHtml: Theme and auth session are strong Context candidates because they are shared values with clear tree-wide consumers. The checkout flow is a good reducer candidate because transitions matter. The live websocket feed often belongs in an external store or dedicated subscription layer because React does not naturally own that source of truth.

blocks.title: Mastery recap
items: Context is not a replacement for good ownership.
Reducers make transitions explicit.
External stores need stable snapshots and subscriptions.
Choose the boundary based on who owns the data and how often it changes.

qa.question: When is Context a good fit in React?
qa.answerHtml: Context is a good fit for shared low-frequency values like theme, locale, or session data that many descendants need to read.

qa.question: When should you use useReducer instead of multiple useState calls?
qa.answerHtml: Use a reducer when one domain has multiple related transitions and you want explicit action-based updates that are easier to reason about.

qa.question: What problem does useSyncExternalStore solve?
qa.answerHtml: It provides a safe subscription contract for state that lives outside React, with a stable snapshot getter and subscribe function that work correctly with concurrent rendering.

qa.question: When would an external store be a better fit than Context?
qa.answerHtml: An external store is often better when many parts of the UI need selective subscriptions, when data updates frequently, or when the source of truth already exists outside React.

sections.intro: Modern React gives the UI a way to wait, defer, and stage asynchronous work without turning every component into manual loading-state code.

blocks.title: Suspense is a display boundary, not a fetch library
blocks.html:
<Suspense> lets React show a fallback while a child is still loading, but only Suspense-enabled sources can actually trigger it. That includes lazy-loaded component code, cached Promises read with use(), and framework integrations built to suspend.

Suspense does not detect data fetched inside an Effect or a click handler. It is about how loading is displayed and coordinated in the UI, not a magical replacement for your data architecture.

blocks.title: useTransition vs useDeferredValue

items.label: useTransition
items.html: Marks a state update as non-urgent. Best when you own the setter and want the whole update to happen in the background.

items.label: useDeferredValue
items.html: Lets one part of the UI lag behind another value. Best when you want a subtree to keep showing stale content while fresh content loads.

items.label: Shared goal
items.html: Both help avoid jarring Suspense fallbacks for already revealed content, but they solve different scheduling problems.

blocks.title: Showing stale results while fresh results load
blocks.caption: The input updates urgently, while the results subtree can lag behind without hiding already revealed content.

blocks.title: How React stages async UI work
steps: Urgent updates like typing should stay responsive and commit first.
A transition or deferred value marks slower work as non-urgent.
If a subtree suspends, the nearest Suspense boundary decides whether to reveal a fallback or keep already shown content visible.
Nested boundaries let you control which content loads together and which content reveals progressively.

blocks.title: Trap: wrapping a text input update in a Transition
blocks.html: Controlled inputs are urgent. If you put the input's own value update inside a Transition, typing can feel broken because React may intentionally defer it. Keep the input state urgent and defer the expensive subtree instead.

blocks.title: Drill: choose Suspense, Transition, or deferred value
blocks.prompt: A search box should update immediately, but its result list is expensive and may suspend while new data loads. Which React tools fit the problem best?
blocks.answerHtml: Keep the text input state urgent. Wrap the results area in a Suspense boundary and use useDeferredValue for the query, or use a Transition around navigation-style updates when you own the setter for the slower subtree. Do not defer the controlled input itself.

blocks.title: Mastery recap
items: Suspense is a UI boundary, not a fetch library.
Transitions mark non-urgent updates when you own the setter.
Deferred values let a subtree lag behind while urgent UI stays responsive.
Suspense-enabled sources, not arbitrary Effects, are what actually trigger Suspense.

qa.question: What is Suspense for in React?
qa.answerHtml: Suspense coordinates how loading states are displayed when part of the tree is not ready yet. It is a display boundary for waiting.

qa.question: What is the difference between useTransition and useDeferredValue?
qa.answerHtml: useTransition marks updates as non-urgent when you own the setter. useDeferredValue lets a subtree lag behind a changing value so urgent UI can stay responsive.

qa.question: What kinds of loading sources can trigger Suspense?
qa.answerHtml: Suspense-enabled sources such as lazy-loaded code, cached Promises read with use(), and supported framework integrations can trigger Suspense. Data fetched inside an Effect does not automatically do so.

qa.question: What does the use() API do in React?
qa.answerHtml: use() lets React read certain resources such as a Promise or context during render in Suspense-enabled flows, so the component can suspend until the value is ready.

sections.intro: Forms sit at the boundary between browser behavior, React state, and mutation flows.

blocks.title: Controlled vs uncontrolled vs action-driven forms
blocks.html:
Choose controlled inputs when the UI must react to every keystroke. Choose uncontrolled inputs when native form behavior and a final submit read are enough. Choose action-driven forms when the mutation flow itself is the center of the design and you want React to coordinate pending state and optimistic feedback.

Modern React adds useActionState, useFormStatus, and useOptimistic so forms and mutations can be modeled as Actions instead of as a pile of ad-hoc loading flags.

blocks.title: useActionState vs useFormStatus vs useOptimistic

items.label: useActionState
items.html: Stores the result of an Action, queues calls sequentially, and exposes an isPending flag for that action state.

items.label: useFormStatus
items.html: Reads the submission status of a parent form. Best for nested submit buttons or status text inside the form tree.

items.label: useOptimistic
items.html: Shows an expected temporary result while an Action is pending. It must be updated inside an Action or Transition.

blocks.title: An action-driven form with pending state
blocks.caption: useFormStatus reads the parent form state from a nested child, while useActionState owns the action result.

blocks.title: How an action-driven form submission flows
steps: The form dispatches an Action through an action prop or a transition.
React treats the action as pending and exposes status information.
useActionState receives the previous state and the payload, then returns the next state.
useOptimistic can temporarily show the expected result until the canonical state catches up.

blocks.title: Trap: calling useFormStatus beside the form it should track
blocks.html: useFormStatus only reports the status of a parent form. If you call it in the same component that renders the form instead of in a nested child inside the form, pending will never reflect that form's submission state.

blocks.title: Drill: which form model fits?
blocks.prompt: A profile form validates on each keystroke, a search bar only needs a final submit read, and a shopping cart button should update immediately while the server confirms. Which React form pattern fits each case best?
blocks.answerHtml: The profile form fits controlled inputs because the UI reacts to each change. The search bar can be uncontrolled if you only need the final submit value. The shopping cart button fits an action-driven flow with useActionState plus useOptimistic for instant feedback.

blocks.title: Mastery recap
items: Controlled and uncontrolled inputs are both valid tools.
Action-driven forms model mutations explicitly.
useFormStatus reads a parent form's submission state from inside the form tree.
Optimistic UI still needs a canonical source of truth and a rollback path.

qa.question: What is the difference between controlled and uncontrolled inputs?
qa.answerHtml: Controlled inputs read their value from React state and update through handlers. Uncontrolled inputs let the DOM keep the current value until you read it through a ref or form submission.

qa.question: What does useActionState do?
qa.answerHtml: useActionState stores the result of an Action, gives you an action function to dispatch, and exposes an isPending flag for that flow.

qa.question: What does useFormStatus do?
qa.answerHtml: useFormStatus reads the submission status of a parent form from inside the form subtree, which is especially useful for nested submit buttons and pending indicators.

qa.question: What is useOptimistic for?
qa.answerHtml: useOptimistic lets the UI show a temporary expected result while an Action is still pending, then reconcile with the canonical data when the real update finishes.

sections.intro: Performance work starts with render behavior, identity, and state placement before manual memoization enters the conversation.

blocks.title: Performance is mostly about render shape
blocks.html:
React performance work usually begins with render shape: where state lives, how often subtrees re-render, whether identities stay stable, and whether expensive work is repeated too often. Most slow React apps are not fixed first by adding memo APIs everywhere.

Move state closer to where it is used, split expensive subtrees, preserve stable identities where it matters, and profile before optimizing.

blocks.title: Manual memoization vs compiler optimization

items.label: Manual memoization
items.html: useMemo, useCallback, and memo give explicit control, but they add code and can be applied incorrectly.

items.label: Compiler optimization
items.html: React Compiler is a build-time optimizer that can automatically apply memoization based on its analysis of components and hooks.

items.label: Real-world stance
items.html: Use the compiler when your stack supports it, but keep measurement, ownership, and render boundaries as the primary skills.

blocks.title: React Compiler is a build-time optimizer
blocks.caption: React Compiler can optimize component and hook work at build time, and directives like 'use no memo' are targeted escape hatches rather than a default style.

blocks.title: Optimization order that usually pays off
steps: Measure first so you know whether rendering, data size, or layout work is actually slow.
Fix state placement and render boundaries before adding caching.
Reduce repeated expensive calculations and unnecessary child renders.
Use compiler or manual memoization where profiling shows it matters, then re-measure.

blocks.title: Trap: assuming the compiler replaces profiling
blocks.html: The compiler can optimize many render paths, but it does not fix slow APIs, bad list virtualization, oversized payloads, or poor state boundaries by itself. Performance still starts with understanding where time is being spent.

blocks.title: Drill: what should you optimize first?
blocks.prompt: A dashboard feels slow after typing in a filter box. Every keystroke re-renders a large table, a sidebar, and summary cards. What is the first React-focused optimization conversation you should have before adding memo hooks everywhere?
blocks.answerHtml: Start by looking at state placement and render boundaries. The filter input state may be too high in the tree, causing unrelated regions to re-render. Split the expensive table subtree, keep urgent input updates local, and profile before reaching for memoization.

blocks.title: Mastery recap
items: Measure before optimizing.
Fix ownership, identity, and render boundaries before adding memoization.
React Compiler is a build-time optimizer, not a replacement for architecture or profiling.
Manual memoization still exists as a precise escape hatch when needed.

qa.question: What causes a React component to re-render?
qa.answerHtml: A component re-renders when its own state changes, when relevant parent work re-runs its subtree, when consumed context changes, or when the root updates that part of the tree.

qa.question: What is the difference between React.memo, useMemo, and useCallback?
qa.answerHtml: React.memo memoizes a component render based on props. useMemo memoizes a computed value. useCallback memoizes a function reference.

qa.question: What does React Compiler change in day-to-day development?
qa.answerHtml: It can automatically optimize many render paths that used to need manual memoization, which reduces boilerplate. But developers still need good boundaries, state placement, and measurement.

qa.question: What should you optimize before adding memoization hooks everywhere?
qa.answerHtml: Start with profiling, state placement, render boundaries, and expensive repeated work. Memoization is more effective after the overall render shape is already sensible.

sections.intro: Server Components and directives live at the boundary between React and the framework or bundler that hosts it.

blocks.title: There is no directive for Server Components
blocks.html:
Server Components are the default on the server side of a React Server Components app. The directives are about boundaries and functions, not about opting a file into being a Server Component. 'use client' marks a client boundary in the module graph, and 'use server' marks server functions that can be called from client-side code.

This distinction matters because many explanations incorrectly imply that Server Components require a directive in the same way Client Components do. They do not.

blocks.title: 'use client' vs 'use server' vs Server Components

items.label: 'use client'
items.html: Marks a module and its transitive dependencies as client code in an app that uses React Server Components.

items.label: 'use server'
items.html: Marks server-side functions that can be called from client-side code through a compatible framework.

items.label: Server Components
items.html: Components rendered on the server side of the RSC architecture. They are part of the render tree, not a standalone directive mode.

blocks.title: A clean server-client split
blocks.caption: The server-rendered page can fetch data and avoid shipping extra client code, while the client boundary owns the interactive behavior.

blocks.title: How the server-client boundary works
steps: The framework builds a module graph and respects 'use client' boundaries.
Server-rendered modules can fetch data, access the file system, or use secrets on the server side.
Client-marked modules are bundled for the browser and can use state, event handlers, and browser APIs.
Props crossing from server-rendered output into client components must be serializable.

blocks.title: Trap: calling every server feature a Server Action
blocks.html: Not every server-side React capability is a Server Action. Server Functions marked with 'use server' can be used in multiple ways, and React Server Components are a broader rendering model than form mutation alone. Precision matters here.

blocks.title: Drill: where should this code run?
blocks.prompt: A component reads from the local file system, formats a large product catalog, and renders a small interactive favorites button for each row. Which parts should stay on the server side, and which part should cross a client boundary?
blocks.answerHtml: The file-system read and heavy formatting should stay in the server-rendered part of the tree. The favorites button should cross a client boundary because it needs state and event handlers. Only the interactive slice needs to be client code.

blocks.title: Mastery recap
items: Client components handle interaction and browser APIs.
Server-oriented React features depend on compatible tooling.
There is no directive that says 'this is a Server Component'.
The important boundary is what code must run in the browser versus what can stay on the server side.

qa.question: What does the 'use client' directive actually mark?
qa.answerHtml: It marks a module and its transitive dependencies as client code in an app that uses React Server Components.

qa.question: What does the 'use server' directive do?
qa.answerHtml: It marks server functions that should run on the server side of a compatible React Server Components setup and be callable through framework integration.

qa.question: Do Server Components need a directive?
qa.answerHtml: No. Server Components are the default on the server side of an RSC app. The directives are for client boundaries and server functions.

qa.question: What kind of data can cross the server-client boundary?
qa.answerHtml: Props crossing from server-rendered code into client components need to be serializable in the format the framework supports. You cannot pass arbitrary live server resources or browser-only objects across that boundary.

sections.intro: React quality work includes accessible UI, resilient error handling, predictable hydration, and behavior-first verification.

blocks.title: Behavior-first testing starts from the user surface
blocks.html:
Prefer tests that assert visible behavior, roles, names, and realistic interactions instead of private implementation details. This makes refactors safer because tests stay anchored to what users and assistive technologies actually observe.

In practice, that means asking what should be visible, clickable, focusable, and announced before you ask what internal hook or state variable changed.

blocks.title: Testing layers in a React app

items.label: Component and integration tests
items.html: Best for rendering logic, form behavior, accessibility roles, state transitions, and interaction feedback.

items.label: Network-aware integration tests
items.html: Best for async flows, error states, retries, optimistic updates, and behavior around real request boundaries.

items.label: End-to-end tests
items.html: Best for browser navigation, auth flows, real layout behavior, and the final check that the app works as a whole.

blocks.title: Query the screen the way a user would
blocks.caption: Prefer accessible queries like role and label text over brittle selectors or implementation-specific hooks.

blocks.title: Hydration mismatches are bugs
steps: Server-rendered HTML must match the initial client render output.
If the client renders different content during hydration, React warns because the illusion of a fast server render breaks.
Some mismatches are recoverable, but they are still bugs that should be fixed.
Common causes include browser-only APIs in render logic, different data on server and client, or conditional rendering that diverges before Effects run.

blocks.title: Trap: asserting component internals instead of behavior
blocks.html: Tests that inspect private state, implementation-only class names, or hook wiring break during harmless refactors. If the user-facing behavior is unchanged, the test should usually stay green.

blocks.title: Drill: what should this test assert?
blocks.prompt: A disclosure button opens a help panel. Should the main test assert that a boolean state variable became true, or that the button and panel now expose the right visible and accessible behavior?
blocks.answerHtml: Assert the user-facing behavior: the panel becomes visible, the button reflects the correct accessible state, and keyboard interaction works. The private boolean is an implementation detail.

blocks.title: Mastery recap
items: Test behavior, not internals.
Accessibility and reliability are part of React mastery.
Hydration mismatches should be treated as bugs, not cosmetic warnings.
Accessible queries usually lead to both better tests and better UI.

qa.question: What should you test in a React component?
qa.answerHtml: Test the behavior users and assistive technologies can observe: visible output, roles, names, interactions, loading states, and error states.

qa.question: Why are accessible queries better in Testing Library?
qa.answerHtml: Accessible queries push tests toward the same surface real users and assistive tech rely on, which makes tests more resilient and often improves the accessibility of the UI itself.

qa.question: What causes hydration mismatch errors in React?
qa.answerHtml: Hydration mismatches happen when the client renders different initial output from the server. Common causes include browser-only APIs in render logic, non-deterministic values, and diverging data between server and client.

qa.question: What do Error Boundaries catch?
qa.answerHtml: Error Boundaries catch render-time and lifecycle-time errors in the subtree they wrap. They do not automatically catch event-handler errors or every async failure unless those surface through rendering.

sections.intro: A senior React answer combines core mental models with tool tradeoffs, debugging skill, and clear stack boundaries.

blocks.title: Architecture is mostly about boundaries and ownership
blocks.html: At scale, React architecture comes down to state ownership, component boundaries, async boundaries, and how much of the stack should stay framework-specific versus portable. Tools should be justified by problem shape, not hype.

blocks.title: Choosing ecosystem tools by problem shape

items.label: Routing
items.html: Use the framework router when the app already lives in a framework. Reach for a dedicated router in standalone client apps.

items.label: Server state
items.html: Use framework-native data flows when they fit. Use dedicated server-state libraries when cache lifecycles and client mutations are a core problem.

items.label: Client state
items.html: Prefer local state, then context or reducers, and only then external stores when scale or update patterns demand it.

blocks.title: How to answer senior React architecture questions
steps: Start with ownership: who owns the data, the mutation, and the loading lifecycle?
Draw the boundaries: local UI state, shared client state, server state, and server-client handoff.
Choose tools based on those boundaries instead of naming tools first.
Explain tradeoffs, not just preferences, so the answer sounds architectural rather than tribal.

blocks.title: Trap: picking tools before drawing boundaries
blocks.html: If you start architecture discussions with 'we should use Zustand' or 'we should use React Query' before defining ownership and lifecycle, you risk solving the wrong problem. Senior answers start from boundaries and only then justify tools.

blocks.title: Drill: architecture discussion
blocks.prompt: You are designing a dashboard with interactive filters, background refetching, optimistic mutations, and a handful of modal workflows. Which state should stay local, which should become shared, and where do dedicated libraries earn their keep?
blocks.answerHtml: Keep view-local toggles, drafts, and modal state local. Share only the filter state that multiple regions truly coordinate around. Treat server-fetched entities as server state with clear cache semantics. Bring in dedicated libraries only when the dashboard's fetch and mutation lifecycle or cross-tree subscriptions become expensive to manage with plain React alone.

blocks.title: Mastery recap
items: Strong React architecture starts with ownership and boundaries.
Separate React core from framework and library choices.
Choose tools because they fit a boundary or lifecycle problem.
Senior interview answers sound clearer when they explain tradeoffs, not just favorite libraries.

qa.question: What is JSX in React?
qa.answerHtml: JSX is syntax for describing React elements. It looks like HTML, but it compiles to JavaScript that creates element objects React can render.

qa.question: What is the difference between a React component and a React element?
qa.answerHtml: A component is something React can call, usually a function. A React element is the object description produced by JSX or by that component. DOM nodes only exist later during commit.

qa.question: What is the difference between props and state?
qa.answerHtml: Props are inputs passed from a parent. State is data owned by the component itself. Props are read-only from the child perspective, while state can be updated by the component through React setters.

qa.question: Why does state feel stale right after a setter call?
qa.answerHtml: Because state is a snapshot for the current render. The setter schedules the next render; it does not rewrite the current event handler's local variable.

qa.question: What do keys do in React?
qa.answerHtml: Keys help React identify sibling subtrees across renders. Stable keys preserve the right state for the right item, while changing a key forces React to treat that subtree as a new identity.

qa.question: What is the difference between render and commit?
qa.answerHtml: Render is when React calls components to compute the next tree. Commit is when React applies the required host updates, refs, and DOM changes.

qa.question: What is reconciliation in React?
qa.answerHtml: Reconciliation is React's process for comparing the previous render tree with the next one so it can preserve what should stay and update only what changed.

qa.question: What is the difference between controlled and uncontrolled inputs?
qa.answerHtml: Controlled inputs read their value from React state and update through event handlers. Uncontrolled inputs let the DOM hold the current value and are usually read through refs or form submission.

qa.question: What causes a React component to re-render?
qa.answerHtml: A component re-renders when its own state changes, when its parent re-renders and passes a changed subtree through, when consumed context changes, or when the root re-renders that part of the tree.

qa.question: When should you avoid useEffect?
qa.answerHtml: Avoid it when you are only deriving display data, responding to a direct user event, or resetting UI that can be modeled with keys or better state ownership. Effects are for synchronizing with external systems.

qa.question: What is the difference between useEffect and useLayoutEffect?
qa.answerHtml: Both synchronize with external systems, but useEffect usually runs after the browser paints, while useLayoutEffect runs earlier around layout and paint timing. Use the layout version only when the visual timing actually matters.

qa.question: What is the difference between useRef and state?
qa.answerHtml: Refs persist across renders without triggering a re-render when they change. State participates in rendering and schedules a new render when updated. If the user should see the value change, it usually belongs in state.

qa.question: What is lifting state up?
qa.answerHtml: Lifting state up means moving shared state to the nearest common parent so multiple children can read it and coordinate through a single source of truth.

qa.question: When does React preserve state and when does it reset it?
qa.answerHtml: React preserves state when the same component identity stays in the same render-tree position. It resets state when the component type changes, the key changes, or the subtree identity is otherwise replaced.

qa.question: What are custom hooks for?
qa.answerHtml: Custom hooks let you extract reusable stateful logic without changing the component hierarchy. They share logic, not component instances.

qa.question: What problem do portals solve?
qa.answerHtml: Portals let you render part of a React subtree into a different DOM container while keeping it in the same React tree. They are useful for modals, overlays, and UI that must escape clipping or stacking constraints.

qa.question: When is Context the right tool, and when does it start hurting?
qa.answerHtml: Context is great for low-frequency shared values like theme or locale. It starts hurting when frequently changing values cause many consumers to re-render or when it replaces good state ownership and domain boundaries.

qa.question: When should you use useReducer instead of several useState calls?
qa.answerHtml: Use a reducer when one domain has multiple related transitions and you want explicit action-based updates. It becomes useful when update logic is complex enough that scattered setters get hard to reason about.

qa.question: What problem does useSyncExternalStore solve?
qa.answerHtml: It gives React a concurrency-safe way to subscribe to data that lives outside React, with a stable snapshot getter and a subscribe function that lets React coordinate updates without tearing.

qa.question: What is Suspense for in React?
qa.answerHtml: Suspense lets React coordinate how loading states are displayed when part of the tree is not ready yet. It is a UI boundary for waiting, not a generic fetch library.

qa.question: What is the difference between useTransition and useDeferredValue?
qa.answerHtml: useTransition marks updates as non-urgent when you own the setter. useDeferredValue lets a subtree lag behind a changing value so urgent UI can stay responsive while slower work catches up.

qa.question: What does useActionState do?
qa.answerHtml: useActionState stores the result of an Action, gives you the action function to pass to the form or transition, and exposes an isPending signal for that flow.

qa.question: What does useFormStatus do?
qa.answerHtml: useFormStatus reads the submission status of a parent form from inside the form tree. It is especially useful for nested submit buttons or inline pending UI.

qa.question: What is useOptimistic for?
qa.answerHtml: It lets the UI show the expected result of a pending action immediately, then converge with the real data when the action finishes. It should still be paired with a real source of truth and a failure strategy.

qa.question: What does React Compiler change in day-to-day development?
qa.answerHtml: It can automatically optimize many render paths that used to need manual memoization, which reduces boilerplate. But developers still need good state placement, good boundaries, and measurement when performance matters.

qa.question: What is the difference between React.memo, useMemo, and useCallback?
qa.answerHtml: React.memo memoizes a component render based on props. useMemo memoizes a computed value. useCallback memoizes a function reference. They solve different identity and recomputation problems.

qa.question: What causes hydration mismatch errors in React?
qa.answerHtml: Hydration mismatches happen when the client renders different initial output from what the server produced. Common causes include browser-only APIs in render logic, different data on the client and server, and conditionals that diverge before hydration completes.

qa.question: What do Error Boundaries catch and what do they not catch?
qa.answerHtml: Error Boundaries catch rendering errors, lifecycle errors, and errors in the subtree they wrap. They do not catch event-handler errors, async callback errors, or server-side failures outside that render path unless those are surfaced through the tree.

qa.question: How would you explain Server Components without overpromising what React alone provides?
qa.answerHtml: I would say Server Components are part of the React Server Components model, but they depend on compatible frameworks and bundlers. They let some components run on the server side of that architecture, while client components still handle interaction and browser APIs.

qa.question: What does the 'use client' directive actually mark?
qa.answerHtml: It marks a module and its transitive dependencies as client code in an app that uses React Server Components. It is a boundary in the module graph, not a general-purpose tag for any interactive component explanation.

qa.question: Why do index keys cause real bugs in interactive lists?
qa.answerHtml: Because React may preserve state by position instead of by item identity. When the order changes, state such as input text, focus, or toggled UI can stick to the wrong row.

qa.question: How would you organize state in a large React feature?
qa.answerHtml: Keep transient UI state local, lift only what truly coordinates between siblings, centralize server-state concerns where cache lifecycles matter, and introduce external stores only when update patterns or cross-tree subscriptions justify the extra abstraction.

qa.question: What is a stale closure in React, and how do you fix it?
qa.answerHtml: A stale closure happens when logic reads values captured from an older render instead of the latest ones. Fixes include functional state updates, better dependency handling, moving logic into event handlers, or using patterns like refs or useEffectEvent when the synchronization model truly needs them.

qa.question: How do you decide whether data belongs in local state, Context, an external store, or server state?
qa.answerHtml: Start with ownership. Keep view-local UI data local. Use Context for shared low-frequency values. Use an external store when many subscribers or outside-React ownership make it necessary. Treat fetched entities and mutation lifecycles as server state when cache and synchronization rules matter.

qa.question: What causes a laggy filter dashboard, and what would you optimize first?
qa.answerHtml: The first suspects are state placed too high in the tree, unnecessary wide re-renders, repeated expensive calculations, or oversized list rendering. Start by profiling, then tighten ownership and render boundaries before adding memoization.

qa.question: How do you choose ecosystem tools for a React app without cargo-culting libraries?
qa.answerHtml: Draw the boundaries first: local UI state, shared client state, server state, routing needs, validation needs, and deployment/runtime constraints. Then pick the smallest set of tools that clearly solves those boundaries, instead of adopting libraries because they are fashionable.

qa.question: How would you debug a stale closure bug in a websocket or interval callback?
qa.answerHtml: First confirm that the callback is reading values captured from an older render. Then decide whether the fix is a functional state update, a better dependency model, moving logic into an event path, or using a ref or useEffectEvent when the synchronization truly needs the latest value without recreating the subscription.

qa.question: Why doesn't Suspense show a fallback for data fetched inside useEffect?
qa.answerHtml: Because Suspense reacts to work that suspends during render through Suspense-enabled sources. Data fetched inside an Effect starts after commit, so it does not suspend the render that produced the screen.

qa.question: How do you fix a hydration mismatch caused by reading localStorage during render?
qa.answerHtml: Do not read browser-only APIs during the server render path. Render a deterministic initial value that matches the server output, then read localStorage after hydration in an Effect or behind a client-only boundary and update the UI from there.

qa.question: What is the difference between server-side rendering, hydration, and React Server Components?
qa.answerHtml: Server-side rendering produces HTML on the server. Hydration attaches client-side React behavior to that HTML in the browser. React Server Components are a broader architecture where some components render on the server side and some cross a client boundary, reducing shipped client JavaScript for server-only work.

qa.question: What are common reasons a React app feels slow even when memoization is already used?
qa.answerHtml: The real problem may be state placed too high, huge lists without virtualization, repeated expensive calculations, oversized payloads, synchronous browser work, or badly chosen boundaries. Memoization can hide symptoms without fixing the main cost.

qa.question: When is React.memo harmful or unnecessary?
qa.answerHtml: It is unnecessary when the component is cheap, props always change, or the compiler already handles the useful optimization. It can be harmful when it adds mental overhead, hides a deeper boundary problem, or encourages teams to optimize blindly instead of profiling.

qa.question: How would you split a giant Context that causes re-renders everywhere?
qa.answerHtml: Start by separating unrelated concerns into distinct providers so changes in one domain do not invalidate every consumer. Keep rapidly changing values closer to where they are used or move them to an external store if subscription granularity matters.

qa.question: How do you choose a good Suspense boundary?
qa.answerHtml: A good boundary groups content that should reveal together without making already visible content disappear unnecessarily. It should match the loading story the user can understand, not just arbitrary component lines.

qa.question: What happens if you update state during render?
qa.answerHtml: Updating state during render is usually a bug because render is supposed to stay pure. React may warn, loop, or behave unpredictably because the component is trying to change state while React is still calculating the next UI.

qa.question: How are render purity, compiler optimization, and concurrent rendering related?
qa.answerHtml: All three rely on React being able to treat render as a pure calculation. If render has side effects or hidden mutations, both compiler optimizations and concurrent scheduling become harder or unsafe because React cannot freely replay or optimize that work.

qa.question: How do you optimize very large lists in React?
qa.answerHtml: Use stable keys, keep row state local, avoid needless parent re-renders, and virtualize the list so only visible rows render at once. Large-list performance is usually a rendering-volume problem before it is a memo-hook problem.

qa.question: When should a form stay controlled, and when should it move to uncontrolled or action-driven patterns?
qa.answerHtml: Keep forms controlled when the UI needs to react to each keystroke or validation step. Prefer uncontrolled inputs for simpler submit-only flows. Use action-driven patterns when the mutation lifecycle, pending state, optimistic feedback, and server response are central to the user experience.

qa.question: How would you debug an Effect that keeps reconnecting a websocket?
qa.answerHtml: Inspect what dependencies are changing and whether the Effect is reading changing callbacks or objects that force teardown and resubscription. Then stabilize the boundary correctly with better ownership, extracted configuration, or useEffectEvent for logic that needs fresh values without forcing reconnects.

qa.question: What is tearing in UI state, and how do external stores avoid it?
qa.answerHtml: Tearing is when different parts of the UI observe inconsistent snapshots of shared state during rendering. External stores avoid it in React by exposing a stable snapshot getter and subscription model through useSyncExternalStore.

qa.question: How do you place server and client boundaries to reduce the JavaScript bundle size?
qa.answerHtml: Keep data fetching, heavy formatting, and server-only dependencies on the server side, then carve out the smallest client boundaries that truly need interactivity, browser APIs, or local state. Push the boundary down instead of marking large files as client code by default.

qa.question: How would you answer 'How does React work under the hood?' in an interview?
qa.answerHtml: I would explain that React builds a tree of elements from pure renders, compares that tree to the previous one during reconciliation, schedules work according to priority, and commits only the necessary host updates. Then I would connect that to state snapshots, identity, and why purity matters for optimization and scheduling.

qa.question: What is React?
qa.answerHtml: A declarative, efficient, and flexible JavaScript library for building user interfaces, primarily maintained by Meta (Facebook).

qa.question: What is a component?
qa.answerHtml: Independent and reusable bits of code. They serve the same purpose as JavaScript functions, but work in isolation and return HTML (JSX) via a render function.

qa.question: What are props?
qa.answerHtml: Short for properties. They are read-only components that must be kept pure. They are used to pass data from one component to another.

qa.question: What is state in React?
qa.answerHtml: An object that holds some information that may change over the lifetime of the component. When state changes, the component responds by re-rendering.

qa.question: What is the Virtual DOM?
qa.answerHtml: A lightweight JavaScript representation of the DOM. React updates the Virtual DOM first, compares it to the previous version (diffing), and then applies the minimal necessary changes to the real DOM (reconciliation).

qa.question: What is reconciliation?
qa.answerHtml: The process through which React updates the Browser DOM. When a component�s state changes, React calculates the difference between the new and old Virtual DOMs and only updates the changed nodes.

qa.question: What is the "key" prop?
qa.answerHtml: A special string attribute you need to include when creating lists of elements. Keys help React identify which items have changed, been added, or been removed, optimizing the reconciliation process.

qa.question: What are React Hooks?
qa.answerHtml: Functions that let you "hook into" React state and lifecycle features from function components. They do not work inside classes.

qa.question: What does useState do?
qa.answerHtml: It declares a state variable that you can update directly. It returns a pair: the current state value and a function that lets you update it.

qa.question: What does useEffect do?
qa.answerHtml: It lets you perform side effects in function components, such as data fetching, subscriptions, or manually changing the DOM.

qa.question: What is a functional component vs a class component?
qa.answerHtml: Function components are simpler, writing UI as pure functions. Class components require extending `React.Component` and using `this`. Hooks made function components the modern standard.

qa.question: What is an event in React?
qa.answerHtml: React events are named using camelCase, rather than lowercase. With JSX you pass a function as the event handler, rather than a string.

qa.question: What are synthetic events?
qa.answerHtml: React's cross-browser wrapper around the browser's native event. They have the same interface as native events but work identically across all browsers.

qa.question: How do you conventionally name boolean props?
qa.answerHtml: They are usually named like an assertion (e.g., `isOpen`, `hasLoaded`, `disabled`). Passing a boolean prop without a value defaults it to `true` (e.g., `

qa.question: What is children in React?
qa.answerHtml: A special prop (`props.children`) that allows you to pass components as data to other components, just like nesting standard HTML elements.

qa.question: How do you conditionally render an element?
qa.answerHtml: Using JavaScript logical operators like `&&` (if true, render right side) or the ternary operator `condition ? true : false`.

qa.question: Why is standard `class` attribute written as `className` in React?
qa.answerHtml: Because `class` is a reserved keyword in JavaScript. JSX is transformed into standard JS, requiring the use of the DOM API property `className`.

qa.question: What is a controlled component?
qa.answerHtml: A form element whose value is controlled entirely by React state rather than the DOM.

qa.question: What is an uncontrolled component?
qa.answerHtml: A component where the form data is handled by the DOM itself. You use a `ref` to access the value from the DOM directly when needed.

qa.question: What does preventDefault() do?
qa.answerHtml: It prevents the default action that belongs to the event from occurring. Usually used in form submissions to stop the browser from reloading the page.

qa.question: Why shouldn't you mutate state directly in React?
qa.answerHtml: Because React compares objects by reference. If you mutate state directly, the reference stays the same, and React doesn't know it needs to re-render. Always use the setter function.

qa.question: What is React Context?
qa.answerHtml: A way to pass data through the component tree without having to pass props down manually at every level. Useful for global data like themes or user authentication.

qa.question: Explain the dependency array in useEffect.
qa.answerHtml: If provided, the effect runs only when one of those dependencies has changed since the last render. If empty `[]`, it runs once on mount. If omitted entirely, it runs after EVERY render.

qa.question: What happens if you omit a changing variable from the useEffect dependency array?
qa.answerHtml: The effect might use stale closures (old values of the variable from a previous render), leading to bugs that are very difficult to track down.

qa.question: What is the cleanup function in useEffect?
qa.answerHtml: The function returned by the effect. It runs before the component unmounts AND before the effect runs again on a subsequent re-render. Crucial for removing event listeners or clearing timers.

qa.question: What is useCallback?
qa.answerHtml: A hook that returns a memoized callback function. It's used to prevent functions from being recreated on every render, which is useful when passing callbacks to optimized child components.

qa.question: What is useMemo?
qa.answerHtml: A hook that returns a memoized value. It's used to cache the results of expensive calculations between renders.

qa.question: When should you NOT use useMemo and useCallback?
qa.answerHtml: Don't use them indiscriminately. Memoization has a performance cost (memory allocation and dependency checking). Only use them if the child is wrapped in `React.memo` or if the calculation is genuinely expensive.

qa.question: What is React.memo()?
qa.answerHtml: A Higher Order Component (HOC) that memorizes the rendered output of a component, skipping re-renders if its props haven't changed (shallow comparison).

qa.question: What is useRef?
qa.answerHtml: A hook that returns a mutable ref object whose `.current` property is initialized to the passed argument. It persists for the full lifetime of the component and mutating it does NOT cause a re-render.

qa.question: List modern ways to manage global state in React.
qa.answerHtml: Zustand, Context API (for low-frequency updates), Redux Toolkit, Jotai, Recoil, and using Server State managers like React Query/TanStack Query.

qa.question: Why is using index as a key a bad idea?
qa.answerHtml: If the list order changes (sorting, deleting, inserting), the index stays the same for that position in the DOM. React will get confused, potentially matching wrong state to the wrong UI elements or failing to animate.

qa.question: What is the difference between element and component in React?
qa.answerHtml:
An element is a plain object describing what you want to appear on the screen (`

` or ``). A component is a function or class that accepts input and returns a React element.

qa.question: What is the Custom Hook pattern?
qa.answerHtml: Extracting stateful logic from a component into a reusable JavaScript function whose name starts with "use". They can call other hooks internally.

qa.question: What is StrictMode in React?
qa.answerHtml: A tool for highlighting potential problems in an application. In development mode, it intentionally double-invokes components and lifecycle methods to help find side effects and deprecations.

qa.question: How do you lazy load a component in React?
qa.answerHtml: Using `React.lazy()` combined with `}>`. It allows you to dynamically import a component file only when it is actually needed to be rendered.

qa.question: What are higher-order components (HOCs)?
qa.answerHtml: A function that takes a component and returns a new component, wrapping it with additional injected functionality. Mostly replaced in modern React by custom hooks.

qa.question: What is a render prop?
qa.answerHtml: A technique for sharing code between components using a prop whose value is a function. The component uses that function to determine what to render. (e.g. ` } />`)

qa.question: What is a Portal in React?
qa.answerHtml: It provides a first-class way to render children into a DOM node that exists entirely outside the DOM hierarchy of the parent component (e.g., `createPortal(child, container)`). Perfect for Modals and Tooltips.

qa.question: What is the "forwardRef" API?
qa.answerHtml: A way to let a component take a `ref` they receive and pass it further down ("forward" it) to a child DOM element. Required if a parent needs to measure or focus an inner button of a child component.

qa.question: What is useReducer?
qa.answerHtml: An alternative to `useState`. It is usually preferable when you have complex state logic that involves multiple sub-values or when the next state depends heavily on the previous one.

qa.question: Why might a React update be batched?
qa.answerHtml: React 18+ automatically batches multiple state updates within the same event loop (even inside promises or timeouts) into a single re-render for better performance.

qa.question: What is the difference between shallow rendering and full DOM rendering in testing?
qa.answerHtml: Shallow rendering tests a component in isolation without rendering its children. Full DOM rendering mounts the full tree. Modern testing (RTL) strongly prefers full mounting.

qa.question: How does React Router work under the hood?
qa.answerHtml: It listens to changes in the browser's History API. When the URL changes, it calculates which `` components match the path and renders them, blocking the browser from doing a full page refresh.

qa.question: What is the Context Consumer vs useContext?
qa.answerHtml: useContext is the modern Hook approach used inside a functional component. Context.Consumer is the older render-prop approach required in class components.

qa.question: How do you handle API calls in React components natively?
qa.answerHtml: You traditionally use fetch/axios inside a `useEffect` with an empty dependency array to call the API on mount, updating state on resolution, while managing `isLoading` and `error` states manually.

qa.question: Why is TanStack Query (React Query) recommended over raw useEffect?
qa.answerHtml: It automatically handles caching, background fetching, retries, deduplication, and simplifies loading/error state management significantly over manual useEffects.

qa.question: What is error boundary in React?
qa.answerHtml: A classical component (using `componentDidCatch`) that catches JavaScript errors anywhere in its child component tree, logs those errors, and displays a fallback UI instead of crashing the whole app.

qa.question: What causes the "Warning: Cannot update a component while rendering a different component" error?
qa.answerHtml: It happens when you try to update the state of a parent component directly synchronously inside the render body of a child component, usually by accidentally invoking a prop function instead of passing it by reference.

qa.question: How do you handle routing protection (Protected Routes)?
qa.answerHtml: By creating a wrapper component that checks an authentication token. If valid, it returns the children. If invalid, it uses `` to redirect to the login page.

qa.question: What is code splitting?
qa.answerHtml: Splitting your code into multiple smaller bundles instead of one massive file. React `lazy()` and dynamic imports load those bundles on demand, accelerating initial page load.

qa.question: What is hydration in React?
qa.answerHtml: When React boots up on the client and attaches event listeners to the static HTML that was already sent by a server during Server-Side Rendering (SSR).

qa.question: What is the React 19 Compiler?
qa.answerHtml: An optimizing compiler that automatically memorizes your React code, effectively removing the need for manual `useMemo` and `useCallback` by analyzing component data dependencies at build time.

qa.question: What are React Server Components (RSC)?
qa.answerHtml: A new architecture where components render exclusively on the server at build time or request time. They have ZERO JavaScript sent to the client, cannot use hooks, but can directly access databases.

qa.question: What is the difference between a Server Component and SSR?
qa.answerHtml: SSR renders HTML on the server, but still sends the JS code to the client to hydrate into interactive components. RSC NEVER sends its JS to the client at all, acting purely as structured data.

qa.question: What are Server Actions (React 19)?
qa.answerHtml:
A feature allowing you to define asynchronous functions that execute on the server. You can call these directly from client-side `

` or event handlers without configuring API routes.

qa.question: What is useOptimistic (React 19)?
qa.answerHtml: A hook that lets you eagerly update the UI assuming a server action will succeed, creating instantaneous feedback, while automatically rolling back if the action fails.
